did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically CNN and CNN-LSTM architectures (MobileNet-V2 and InceptionV3), for the automated classification of pigmented skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and comparative evaluation of specific deep learning architectures (MobileNet-V2 and InceptionV3, both with and without LSTM) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing and model training, and reports specific performance results (e.g., InceptionV3+LSTM achieving the highest accuracy), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Deep Learning for Skin Disease Classification: A Comparative Study of CNN and CNN-LSTM Architectures
journal: Journal of Computing and Data Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the automated classification of pigmented skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven diagnostic categories. The authors employed deep learning architectures, specifically MobileNet-V2 and InceptionV3, utilizing transfer learning and advanced preprocessing techniques. To overcome the limitations of traditional CNNs in capturing sequential patterns, the study compared baseline CNN models with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-augmented variants. The evaluation focused on assessing improvements in classification performance, particularly for underrepresented lesion classes, through sequential feature modeling. The results indicated that integrating LSTM enhances contextual feature learning, with the InceptionV3+LSTM model achieving the highest classification accuracy among the tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer with high fatality; ~1100 images (11.0%); underrepresented class where LSTM integration improved detection.
Benign melanocytic tumors; majority class (~6700 images, 66.9%); visually similar to Melanoma causing misclassification.
Common form of skin cancer; ~500 images (5.0%).
Pre-malignant and malignant epidermal tumors; ~300 images (3.0%).
Benign fibrous skin tumors; underrepresented class (~110 images, 1.1%); LSTM-enhanced feature modeling helped handle class imbalance.
Hemangiomas, angiomas, and other vascular types; underrepresented class (~140 images, 1.4%); LSTM integration improved accuracy for this class.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images using the HAM10000 dataset. The study compares baseline CNN models (MobileNet-V2, InceptionV3) with LSTM-augmented variants. The primary metric reported is classification accuracy (72.01% for InceptionV3+LSTM), along with precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC curves (AUC > 0.90).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive dermatoscopic image collection comprising 10,015 high-resolution RGB images of pigmented skin lesions across seven diagnostic classes. It is a public benchmark dataset used for machine learning-based dermatological analysis.

use.training: Used for training and fine-tuning MobileNet-V2 and InceptionV3 baseline CNN models, as well as CNN-LSTM augmented variants.
use.testing: Used for evaluating classification performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) of the proposed models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance across all seven skin lesion categories and identify misclassifications among visually similar classes.
To validate the model's discriminative capability and separability for each class using a one-vs-rest approach.
To quantify the separability of the model, with values exceeding 0.90 indicating excellent performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC curves (AUC).

HAM10000.experiment: Comparative Study of CNN and CNN-LSTM Architectures

InceptionV3 + LSTM.notes: Achieved the highest classification accuracy among all tested configurations. Consistently outperformed other models across precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. AUC values exceeded 0.90 for most classes.

MobileNetV2 + LSTM.notes: Significantly improved from its baseline version. Slightly trailing in accuracy and F1-Score compared to InceptionV3 + LSTM but offers competitive performance with lower computational overhead.

MobileNetV2.notes: Baseline CNN model. Yielded slightly lower performance compared to LSTM-augmented variants.

InceptionV3.notes: Baseline CNN model. Marginal performance compared to InceptionV3 + LSTM (72.01%). Specific accuracy value not explicitly stated in text, but described as outperformed by the LSTM variant.

HAM10000.notes: The integration of LSTM enhanced contextual feature learning, particularly for underrepresented lesion classes (Dermatofibroma and Vascular Lesions). Misclassifications were minimal and predominantly occurred among visually similar classes such as Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi. Confusion matrix analysis showed diagonal dominance indicating high rate of correct predictions. ROC curve analysis validated discriminative capability with high true positive rates.

architecture_used.main_model: InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 (baseline CNNs) and their LSTM-augmented variants (InceptionV3+LSTM, MobileNetV2+LSTM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset, then fine-tuned on HAM10000

InceptionV3.description: Multi-scale feature extraction using Inception modules, factorized convolutions (breaking large filters into smaller ones), auxiliary classifiers, and label smoothing.

MobileNetV2.description: Lightweight architecture using depthwise separable convolutions, inverted residual blocks, and linear bottlenecks.

LSTM_integration.description: LSTM layers added after convolutional blocks to model spatial dependencies embedded in feature maps by sequentially reshaping feature vectors.
LSTM_integration.purpose: Capture long-range spatial dependencies and contextual patterns

training_details.loss_function: Focal loss (mentioned as contributing to performance improvements)

classification_or_output_head.details: Seven diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values to the range [0,1]

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as one of the optimization strategies used during training, but specific techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, color jittering) and their parameters are not reported in the provided text.

conclusions: The integration of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) layers into Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures enhances contextual feature learning and improves classification performance, particularly for underrepresented lesion classes in imbalanced datasets like HAM10000.
The InceptionV3+LSTM model achieved the highest classification accuracy (72.01%) and recall (72.30%) among all tested configurations, outperforming baseline CNNs and MobileNetV2 variants.

InceptionV3's multi-scale convolutional layers are particularly effective at capturing high-level lesion characteristics such as border irregularity, asymmetry, and variegated pigmentation, which are critical for distinguishing melanomas from benign nevi.

MobileNetV2 offers a trade-off between a minor drop in classification performance and significant gains in computational efficiency, making it a strong candidate for mobile or edge-based clinical deployments where low computational overhead is required.

The MobileNetV2+LSTM variant significantly improved performance (accuracy: 71.92%) compared to the baseline MobileNetV2 (69.30%), demonstrating that even lightweight models benefit from sequential spatial modeling.

Misclassifications were minimal and predominantly occurred among visually similar classes, specifically between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi, reflecting the inherent challenge in distinguishing borderline lesions.

The InceptionV3+LSTM model demonstrated excellent separability with Area Under the Curve (AUC) values exceeding 0.90 for most classes, maintaining a favorable balance between sensitivity and specificity even for underrepresented classes like Dermatofibroma and Vascular Lesions.
The study validates the hypothesis that temporal modeling of spatial features improves classification performance in class-imbalanced dermatological datasets.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (NV ~67%) and demographic bias (predominantly European/Caucasian skin types), limiting generalizability to diverse populations.

The paper does not report external validation; results are confined to the HAM10000 benchmark, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability across different imaging devices and skin tones.

The maximum classification accuracy achieved was 72.01% (InceptionV3+LSTM), which is relatively low for a multi-class problem with 7 classes, suggesting limited discriminative power for clinically challenging or underrepresented classes.
The paper states 'No data is available in this article' in the declarations, meaning the code and processed data splits are not publicly reproducible by the authors.
The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the significance of performance differences between models.
The input resolution was reduced to 128x128 pixels, which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for distinguishing visually similar lesions like Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi.

The paper acknowledges that misclassifications predominantly occur between Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevi, highlighting the persistent difficulty in distinguishing malignant from benign lesions even with advanced architectures.
The authors declare no external funding and no conflict of interest, but the lack of independent validation by a third party or clinical trial limits the evidence strength for clinical deployment.
